CAP works in the real world, how it influences modern data architectures, and why databases like Postgres, Cassandra, DynamoDB, Kafka, and"},{"title":"CAP Theorem in Real-World Scenarios - DEV Community","url":"https://dev.to/codemasheen/cap-theorem-in-real-world-scenarios-559f","content":"The CAP theorem explains the trade-offs between three important factors in distributed systems: Consistency (C), Availability (A), and Partition Tolerance (P). This theorem states that when a network partition occurs in a distributed system, you can only achieve two out of the three properties at the same time: Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance. However, in 2012, Dr. Brewer clarified that the \"two out of three\" concept can be somewhat misleading because system designers only need to sacrifice consistency or availability as partition management and recovery techniques exist. ## Availability + Partition Tolerance (AP). PostgreSQL maintains Consistency and Partition Tolerance, ensuring the integrity of transactions, especially for critical operations like reservations and payments. Redis enhances Availability and Partition Tolerance, allowing real-time data retrieval (e.g., current parking availability) even during network issues. Key takeaway: Eventual consistency allows systems to be available during partitions while still achieving correctness over time. Designing distributed systems involves balancing availability, consistency, and partition tolerance based on product requirements."},{"title":"A Simple Guide to CAP Theorem: Real-world Examples and Practical Tips | by Abhishek Ranjan | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@abhishekranjandev/a-simple-guide-to-cap-theorem-real-world-examples-and-practical-tips-62fae0f0b536","content":"When building a system like YouTube, which is a massive video-sharing platform, it’s essential to apply the CAP Theorem to ensure the right balance of consistency, availability, and partition tolerance. A distributed data store like Amazon DynamoDB or Apache Cassandra would be suitable for this purpose, as they are designed to handle high levels of read and write traffic while maintaining availability and partition tolerance. Although the priority for YouTube-like systems is availability and partition tolerance, it’s still essential to minimize data inconsistencies. In summary, when building a YouTube-like system, applying CAP Theorem involves prioritizing availability and partition tolerance while minimizing data inconsistencies. By selecting the right data store, designing for scalability and redundancy, optimizing for inconsistencies, and leveraging caching and CDNs, you can create a distributed system capable of handling the demands of a large-scale video-sharing platform. By carefully considering the properties most important to your application and choosing the appropriate data store, you can create a distributed system that balances consistency, availability, and partition tolerance."},{"title":"CAP Theorem Explained: Consistency, Availability & Partition ...","url":"https://www.bmc.com/blogs/cap-theorem/","content":"# CAP Theorem Explained: Consistency, Availability & Partition Tolerance. The CAP theorem is a belief from theoretical computer science about distributed data stores that claims, in the event of a network failure on a distributed database, it is possible to provide either consistency or availability—but not both. But the CAP theorem maintains that when a distributed database experiences a network failure, you can provide either consistency or availability. Consistency and availability in CAP theorem. ┄ [obs]      [EXEC s2] ✓ The CAP theorem identifies three key properties in distributed systems: **Consistency (C)** (all nodes see the same data at the same time), **Availability (A)** (every request receives a response, even during failures), and **Partition Tolerance (P)** (the system continues to operate despite network partitions). The core trade-off is that **during a network partition, a system can only guarantee two of the three properties**—typically, **consistency vs. availability** (since partition tolerance is essential for distributed systems).  

- **CA (Consistency + Availability)**: Traditional databases like **PostgreSQL** (when not distributed) prioritize consistency and availability, assuming no network partitions.  
- **AP (Availability + Partition Tolerance)**: Systems like **Amazon DynamoDB** prioritize availability and partition tolerance, allowing eventual consistency during partitions (e.g., for scalable, real-time applications).  
- **CP (Consistency + Partition Tolerance)**: **Apache Cassandra** prioritizes consistency and partition tolerance, ensuring data accuracy even during network issues, though this may reduce availability temporarily.

┄ [thought]  [REFLECT 1] ✓ SATISFIED SATISFIED: The answer fully addresses the CAP theorem by explaining each component (C, A, P), the core trade-off (choosing between consistency/availability during partitions), and provides real-world examples for CA (PostgreSQL), AP (DynamoDB), and CP (Cassandra).
